project_description.text: The 1980 Recommended Renewable Resources Program is a U.S. Forest Service plan, prepared under the Forest and Rangeland Renewable Resources Planning Act of 1974 (as amended by the National Forest Management Act of 1976), guiding management of the Nation's forest and rangeland resources from 1981 through 2030. It covers four major Forest Service responsibilities—management of about 187 million acres of National Forest System lands, State and Private Forestry assistance, Research, and Human Resource Programs—organized around 12 program elements including recreation, wilderness, wildlife and fish, range, timber, water, minerals, and supporting activities. The Program is presented as a range between a High Bound, with substantially increased outputs and environmental protection, and a Low Bound, providing more modest outputs under constrained federal spending.

affected_community.text: The Program affects users and communities nationwide, with the strongest effects in rural and nonmetropolitan areas dependent on Forest Service outputs—particularly timber-dependent communities in the Pacific Northwest and South, ranching communities in the West, and areas experiencing mineral development 'boom town' conditions. Other affected groups include nonindustrial private forest landowners, State forestry agencies, recreation users (including urban residents), county and local governments receiving federal payments, and consumers facing rising prices for wood, forage, and water products. Special attention is given to Native Americans (especially those with fishing rights in Alaska and California), minorities, low-income families using rangelands, and youth, elderly, and disadvantaged people served through Human Resource Programs.

alternatives_overview.text: Five Alternative Program Directions were evaluated: Alternative 1 (highest market and nonmarket outputs), Alternative 2 (minimum custodial program), Alternative 3 (moderate balanced multiple-use, similar to the 1975 Program), Alternative 4 (low NFS market outputs with high nonmarket emphasis and expanded State/private production), and Alternative 5 (continuation of current trends). A sixth alternative, 4a, was added in response to public comment to combine high nonmarket outputs with maintained commodity production through intensive management on a smaller commercial land base. The Recommended Program was constructed as a range between a High Bound and a Low Bound, with the High Bound generally falling near Alternatives 1, 3, or 4 by element, and the Low Bound approximating Alternative 5; departures from nondeclining even-flow timber harvest were also considered.

environmental_impact.text: Both Program Bounds and most alternatives would meet Clean Water Act water quality goals by 2000–2005 and national ambient air quality standards by 1995, with short-term localized impacts from vegetation treatment, road building, and mining. Under the High Bound and higher-output alternatives, wildlife and fish habitat would improve significantly, with most threatened and endangered species populations stable or increasing, though old-growth-dependent species such as Sitka black-tailed deer and marten in Alaska would decline due to timber harvest. Lower-output alternatives reduce short-term disturbance but increase long-term wildfire risk from fuel buildup and provide less habitat improvement, while mineral development and timber harvest would cause visual and soil impacts that cannot be fully mitigated. Failure to meet projected demand could also drive rising prices, increased imports, and greater use of nonrenewable substitute materials with their own environmental costs.

public_response.text: Public participation was extensive: a 1977 comment period generated over 2,000 pages of input, and review of the March 1979 draft documents drew more than 1,600 respondents through nationwide public meetings, with about 70 percent of written comments from unaffiliated individuals. Roughly 45 percent of respondents preferred one of the five alternatives, with Alternatives 3 and 4 most favored in the East, Alternative 3 in much of the West, and Alternative 1 strongly preferred in Alaska, while Alternatives 2 and 5 received the least support. Comments led to adding Alternative 4a, identifying new policy issues, increasing emphasis on wildlife and fish, water quality, wilderness, and recreation, continuing restrictions on log exports from Federal lands, and shaping cooperative State and Private Forestry programs. Congressional hearings by the Subcommittee on Forests with over 150 witnesses also contributed to the planning process.

value.name: Alternative 1
value.description: Gives high priority to both production and non-production elements and would be the most expensive option, retaining the highest monetary benefit. Considered unrealistic because the higher production levels outlined for all elements are not believed to be attainable.

value.name: Alternative 2
value.description: A minimal program that would not meet the increasing needs and demands placed on National Forest lands. Recommended for wilderness in combination with Alternative 3.

value.name: Alternative 3
value.description: Provides a relatively good multiple-use balance between production and non-production uses, offering moderate levels of market and non-market resources. Recommended for recreation, wildlife and fish, range, timber, water, minerals, and human and community development.

value.name: Alternative 4
value.description: Shifts the emphasis of resource production to private lands as much as possible. Questioned for feasibility given that the majority of western lands are federally administered, though its emphasis on increased timber production on state and private lands is partially favored.

value.name: Alternative 5
value.description: Provides a moderate level of market resources and a low level of non-market resources. Would not meet the needs or demands for fish and wildlife habitat improvement, range, and timber.

themes.justification: The environmental_impact field highlights significant wildlife and fish habitat outcomes including threatened species and old-growth-dependent species, while the project_description and alternatives_overview describe a federally mandated multi-decade resource management program governing timber, minerals, range, and water across 187 million acres under statutory authority.

location.note: Nationwide programmatic EIS for USDA Forest Service renewable resources program covering all National Forest System lands, State and private forestry, and research across the entire US.

agency_preparers.name: Bob Wray
agency_preparers.role: Leader, Information Services, North Central Forest Experiment Station

agency_preparers.name: Thomas E. Hamilton
agency_preparers.role: Director, Resources Program and Assessment, USDA Forest Service
